text.value:
Plinky runs entirely in your browser — nothing to install, no account, and everything you do stays on your own device.

You can play three ways: plug in a MIDI keyboard, use your computer keyboard (the home page shows which letters map to which keys), or tap the on-screen piano. Pick a piece, press a key, and you are already practising.

The Getting-started checklist on the home page walks you through the first steps and ticks itself off as you go.

The home page is your launch pad. The Today panel gathers what matters right now: the daily challenge, pieces due for review, and the next step of an assignment you are working through.

Below it, the explore cards lead to the library, assignments, composing, and the board. The keyboard at the top is real — tap a key and it plinks.

The play page is where you practise a piece. Play mode listens to what you play and colours each note green, amber, or red as you go; Listen plays the piece to you and hands back to Practice without losing your place; Ear and Fingering are training modes you can switch to in the mode bar.

Use the toolbar to set the tempo, loop a tricky passage, turn the metronome on, or go full screen. Finished runs are kept as takes — replay them, race your own ghost, or save a video.

Every run is graded, and a piece checks off as learned once you have played it well.

The library holds every piece you can play: the built-in catalogue of Creative-Commons music, generated finger exercises, and anything you import yourself. Every piece credits its composer, source, and licence.

Filter by kind, grade, favourites, or what is due for review; star the pieces you love so they are easy to find. Import your own MusicXML files via Import — they stay on your device.

Once a day, Plinky picks one short piece as the daily challenge — the same piece for everyone, so results are comparable. Play it and you get a Wordle-style result card you can share without giving anything away.

There are no streaks and no punishments: miss a day and nothing is lost. The calendar simply shows the days you played, and your stats accumulate.

Compose turns your playing into notation. Hit record, play, and watch the staff write itself; checkpoint whenever you like a take.

When it sounds right, share it as a link or export it as MIDI or MusicXML — an exported piece can be imported back into the library and practised like any other.

An assignment is an ordered practice list — built by a teacher or by you. The first tab lists your assignments; the second is the builder, where you search the catalogue, add pieces, drag titles into order, and give each an optional target tempo and note, plus a description for the whole set.

Share an assignment as a link or a file: whoever opens it gets the list, while their progress stays their own. Each piece checks off as it is learned, and the home page always points at your next step.

The You page is your progress in one place: where you stand on the eight-grade ladder, the stars you have earned, your skill rating, and your lifetime stats.

Grades work like a roadmap — each one is a set of pieces and exercises at that level, and moving up means playing them well. Nothing decays into punishment; it just shows what is fresh and what could use a revisit.

Pieces you have learned come due again after a while — that spacing is what makes them stick. The review session deals you the due pieces one at a time, like flashcards for your fingers.

You can also see what is due from the library's "Due now" filter, or from the Today panel on the home page.

Settings covers how you play and how Plinky looks: connect a MIDI keyboard, customise the computer-keyboard mapping, measure your hand size so fingering suggestions fit your reach, and switch the theme.

Everything lives on this device only. The danger zone at the bottom can reset Plinky to a blank slate — that deletes all your local data, so it asks first.
